title: Ranking Metrics: Extending Acceptability and Performance Indexes
summary: This paper develops an axiomatic framework for ranking metrics, a general class of functionals for evaluating and ordering financial or insurance positions. Unlike traditional risk-adjusted performance measures-such as the Sharpe ratio, RAROC, or Omega-that express reward per unit of risk, ranking metrics assign each position a performance level rather than a normalized return. Relying on monotonicity and a new property called cash-quasiconcavity, we derive representation results linking ranking metrics to families of acceptance sets and risk measures, extending the theory of acceptability indices. Classical ratios arise as special cases, while new examples-based on expected-loss, Lambda-quantile, and bibliometric indices-illustrate the framework's flexibility. Empirical applications to portfolio ranking and climate-risk insurance demonstrate its practical relevance.

Ranking Metrics: Extending Acceptability and Performance Indexes
Asmerilda Hitaj a, Elisa Mastrogiacomo b, Ilaria Peri c, Marcelo Righi d
aUniversità dell’Insubria, Dipartimento di Economia, via M onte Generoso 71, 21100, Varese, Italy

cBirkbeck Business School, Birkbeck University of London, M alet St, Bloomsbury, London, WC1E

Abstract
This paper develops an axiomatic framework for ranking metr ics, a general class of func-
tionals for evaluating and ordering ﬁnancial or insurance p ositions. Unlike traditional
risk-adjusted performance measures—such as the Sharpe rat io, RAROC, or Omega—that
express reward per unit of risk, ranking metrics assign each position a performance level
rather than a normalized return. Relying on monotonicity an d a new property called
cash-quasiconcavity, we derive representation results li nking ranking metrics to families of
acceptance sets and risk measures, extending the theory of a cceptability indices. Classi-
cal ratios arise as special cases, while new examples—based on expected-loss, Λ-quantile,
and bibliometric indices—illustrate the framework’s ﬂexi bility. Empirical applications to
portfolio ranking and climate-risk insurance demonstrate its practical relevance.
Keywords: acceptability indexes; cash-quasiconcavity; Λ-VaR; portfolio performances
1. Introduction
Comparing the performance of alternative positions or inst itutions is a fundamental
task in ﬁnance, insurance, and related ﬁelds. Classical ris k-adjusted performance measures,
including the Risk-Adjusted Return on Capital (RAROC) (Zai k et al., 1996), the Omega
ratio, Keating and Shadwick (2002), and the Gain–Loss Ratio (GLR), Bernardo and Ledoit
(2000), provide alternative ways of assessing performance by expressing returns relative to
a speciﬁc notion of risk, thereby enabling consistent compa rison of investment alternatives.
Despite their practical appeal, these indices remain conce ptually fragmented, having arisen
as heuristic tools reﬂecting speciﬁc interpretations of ri sk and reward.
A signiﬁcant step toward theoretical uniﬁcation was achiev ed by Cherny and Madan
(2009) through the notion of acceptability indices, which a ssign each position an accept-
ability level corresponding to the smallest performance th reshold under which the posi-
tion is considered acceptable. This axiomatic framework em beds classical ratios within

a broader and uniﬁed structure and shows how properties such as monotonicity, follow
naturally from a reward-to-risk interpretation. This appr oach was further extended by
Rosazza Gianin and Sgarra (2013), who relaxed the scale inva riance property and de-
veloped a dynamic framework based on g-expectations. Subsequently, Peri (2012) and
Frittelli et al. (2016a) introduced a minimal deﬁnition for acceptability indices as mono-
tone and quasi-concave maps, providing a dual representati on analogous to the risk mea-
sure framework in Drapeau and Kupper (2013). Recent extensi ons relax the quasiconcavity
property in favour of the more general star-shapedness (Rig hi, 2024).
Although the acceptability index framework represents a ma jor theoretical advance, its
broader potential remains largely underexplored. Most stu dies continue to interpret per-
formance as a normalized measure of reward or a monetary valu e, which limits applicability
in settings such as regulatory stress testing, climate-ris k assessment, or ESG benchmarking
— where evaluation focuses on meeting speciﬁc thresholds or ranking levels rather than
monetary or risk-adjusted performance.
This observation motivates the main contribution of this pa per that is the introduction
of a broader concept which we refer to as ranking metric. A ranking metric is a functional
that assigns to each random outcome a positive real number re presenting its rank, score, or
degree of acceptability, without requiring a reward-to-risk interpretation. It is characterized
by two properties: monotonicity, ensuring that better outc omes are never penalized, and
cash–quasiconcavity (or quasiconcavity at constants), wh ich requires that mixing a random
outcome with a certain one cannot lower its rank below the min imum of its components
— an idea that resembles the concave counterpart of quasi-st ar-shapedness by Han et al.
(2024). This minimal and economically meaningful mixing co ndition generalizes the tra-
ditional quasiconcavity and star-shapedness assumptions , allowing for a broader class of
ranking rules that extend beyond classical performance mea sures and acceptability indices.
While mathematically these metrics correspond to quasi-co ncave-star-shaped acceptability
indices, we propose this shift in terminology to enhance int erpretability. Under this frame-
work, performance can be assessed through ordinal rankings or qualitative levels rather
than being restricted to normalized rewards or monetary val ues, thereby accommodating
policy-driven objectives that extend beyond purely ﬁnanci al magnitudes.
The remainder of the paper is structured as follows. In Secti on 2, we introduce the deﬁ-
nition of ranking metric, study the property of cash-quasic oncavity and explore its relation-
ship with other established properties in the performance m easurement literature. Our aim
is also to clarify the roles of quasi-star-shapedness and st ar-shapedness, properties recently
introduced into the theory of risk measures and acceptabili ty indices (Castagnoli et al.,
2022; Han et al., 2024; Righi, 2024). Then, in Section 3, we pr ovide a uniﬁed axiomatic
framework for ranking metrics. We show that any monotone, ca sh-quasiconcave ranking
metric admits representations in terms of decreasing famil ies of cash-convex acceptance
sets and increasing families of cash-quasiconvex risk meas ures. This enables the construc-
tion of new performance measures that allow for deeper compa risons across portfolios,
institutions, and contexts. In Section 4, we present severa l examples of ranking metrics,
2
encompassing both classical reward-to-risk performance r atios (such as RAROC, GLR,
and Omega) and newly constructed metrics generated by famil ies of risk measures and
representing monetary values — including expected-loss an d certainty-equivalent function-
als and Λ-quantiles. We also identify the conditions under which the se ranking metrics
satisfy cash-(sub or sup)-additivity properties. Particu lar attention is devoted to a novel
class of ranking metrics inspired by bibliometric indices ( e.g., the h-index and related mea-
sures), discussed in Subsection 4.3. Finally, Section 5 ill ustrates the framework through
empirical analyses based on real data. Although the concept of a ranking metric is general
and applicable across various domains, our focus is on ﬁnanc ial performance and climate-
risk resiliance. The ﬁrst application compares portfolio r ankings obtained from diﬀerent
metrics, examining their interpretation and implications for practitioners. We show that
identical datasets can yield markedly diﬀerent orderings d epending on the chosen metric,
underscoring the economic relevance of selecting a ranking rule consistent with speciﬁc
performance objectives or constraints. A complementary an alysis compares European re-
gions’ climate-risk-related loss exposures and their insu rance implications, while a ﬁnal
study explores optimal portfolio allocation under alterna tive ranking metrics.
2. Ranking metrics: basic deﬁnitions and related propertie s
Let (Ω, F, P) be a probability space, and let L∞ := L∞(Ω, F, P) denote the space of
F-measurable random variables that are P-almost surely ( P-a.s.) bounded. Each X ∈
L∞ represents the proﬁt-and-loss of a risky ﬁnancial position or the (negative) loss of an
insurance contract. Unless stated otherwise, all equaliti es and inequalities are understood
P-almost surely. Throughout this paper, we will denote by X a subset of L∞ and by
r a functional from X with values in [0, +∞]. We will indicate by supp (r) the support
of the functional r : X → [0, +∞], which, given the non-negativity of r, is deﬁned by
supp(r) := {X ∈ X : r(X) > 0}. We will also adopt the convention sup{∅} = 0 and
sup {R+} = +∞.
We propose a deﬁnition of a performance ranking metric or simply ranking metric that
meets essential criteria for eﬀectively ranking ﬁnancial a nd insurance performances.
Deﬁnition 1. A map r : X ⊆ L∞ → [0, ∞] is called a ranking metric if it satisﬁes:
i) (increasing) monotonicity: X ≤ Y =⇒ r(X) ≤ r(Y ), X, Y ∈ X ,
ii) cash-quasiconcavity: r(λX + (1− λ)k) ≥ min{r(X), r(k)}, X ∈ X , k ∈ R, λ ∈ [0, 1].
Unlike traditional performance measures or acceptability indices, ranking metrics as-
sign to a risky ﬁnancial proﬁt-and-loss or an insurance loss a value that does not nec-
essarily correspond to a monetary amount; instead, it may re present a relative level of
performance. The choice of ranking metric may reﬂect invest or preferences, but it must
satisfy monotonicity and cash-quasi-concavity to ensure m eaningful performance compar-
isons between diﬀerent ﬁnancial positions. Monotonicity i s the most natural property as
3
it guarantees that higher ranks reﬂect more attractive port folios. On the other hand,
cash-quasiconcavity captures the structural preference o f asset managers for integrating
risk-free positions within a portfolio. This property gene ralizes standard quasiconcavity
stating that any linear convex combination of a risky asset a nd a deterministic payoﬀ must
yield a rank at least as high as the minimum rank of the two.
Remark 1. Cash-quasiconcavity is a weaker requirement than its class ical counterpart,
as standard quasiconcavity (see Deﬁnition 2) directly impl ies cash-quasiconcavity. Conse-
quently, ranking metrics generalize the deﬁnition of accep tability indices in Peri (2012) and
(Frittelli et al., 2016b), which are only required to be mono tone and quasiconcave. Hence,
they also generalize the original framework of Cherny and Ma dan (2009) which further im-
poses scale invariance, i.e., r(λX) = r(X) for all λ > 0, and the Fatou property for dual
representation.
From a behavioral perspective, we proved in the below propos ition that cash-quasi-
concavity has also decision-theoretic justiﬁcation. This property characterizes managers
who exhibit a preference for diversiﬁcation across risky an d deterministic positions. Specif-
ically, when indiﬀerent between a certain and an uncertain o utcome with respect to the
ranking metric, managers tend to prefer a convex mixture of t he two rather than a full
allocation to the uncertain position. Formally, we assume t hat asset managers evaluate
their investments using a ranking metric r : X → [0, +∞]. The preference relation ⪰
induced by r is then deﬁned as: for any X, Y ∈ X
X ⪰ Y ⇐ ⇒ r(X) ≥ r(Y ).
Consequently, ⪰ represents the ordering of an agent who evaluates positions according to r
and prioritizes those with higher ranks. Under this framewo rk, we demonstrate below that
requiring the ranking metric to satisfy cash-quasi-concav ity is equivalent to assuming that
agents prefer combining risky investments with certain one s, even if they are indiﬀerent to
them in isolation. In other words, integrating a risky posit ion (for instance, a stock) with
a certain payoﬀ (such as a zero coupon bond) results in a rank a t least as high as that of
the risky asset in isolation.
Proposition 1. Let r : L∞ → [0, +∞] be a monotone increasing and L∞-continuous map.
r satisﬁes cash-quasiconcavity if and only if X ≃ k =⇒ λX + (1 − λ)k ⪰ X.
Proof. (⇒) It is clear that cash-quasiconcavity implies the property. (⇐) For the converse,
let r(X) ≥ r(k). Then, by continuity there is m ≤ 0 such that r(X + m) = r(k), i.e.,
X+m ≃ k. Thus, by monotonicity and the implication X+m ≃ k ⇒ λ(X+m)+(1−λ)k) ⪰
X + m we obtain
r(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≥ r(λ(X + m) + (1 − λ)k) ≥ r(X + m) = r(k) = min {r(X), r(k)}.
If r(X) < r (k) then, by continuity there is m ≤ 0 such that r(X) = r(k + m). Following
4
the same argument above we have
r(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≥ r(λX + (1 − λ)(k + m)) ≥ r(X) = min {r(X), r(k)}.
Remark 2. Mathematically, cash-quasi-concavity can be viewed as qua si-concavity at con-
stants. This means r satisﬁes quasiconcavity at k for all k ∈ R, where:
quasiconcavity at k ∈ R : r(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≥ min {r(X), r(k)} , X ∈ X , λ ∈ [0, 1].
Alternatively, this property can be referred to as quasi-st ar-concavity or concave-quasi-
star-shapedness, being the concave analog of quasi-star-c onvexity, simply called quasi-star-
shapedness by Han et al. (2024). Here, we suggest the term cas h-quasiconcavity to enhance
its interpretation within a ﬁnancial context.
Investors may adopt diﬀerent ranking metrics to evaluate th e performance of their
positions according to speciﬁc criteria, which can be forma lized through the following
properties.
Deﬁnition 2. Let r : X ⊆ L∞ → [0, ∞] be a map. The map r may satisfy:
i) cash-concavity: r(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≥ λr(X) + (1 − λ)r(k), X ∈ X , k ∈ R, λ ∈ [0, 1];
ii) concavity: r(λX + (1 − λ)Y ) ≥ λr(X) + (1 − λ)r(Y ), X, Y ∈ X , λ ∈ [0, 1];
iii) quasiconcavity: r(λX + (1 − λ)Y ) ≥ min {r(X), r(Y )} , X, Y ∈ X , λ ∈ [0, 1];
iv) [0,1]-super-linearity: r(λX) ≥ λr(X), X ∈ X , λ ∈ [0, 1].
Remark 3. We suggest the term cash-concavity to enhance interpretabi lity in the ﬁnan-
cial context. From a mathematical standpoint, cash-concav ity, or concavity at constants,
corresponds to the notion of concave-star-shapedness. Thi s property is the concave counter-
part to the star-shapedness (or convex-star-shapedness) d eﬁned by Han et al. (2024), which
generalizes the approach in Castagnoli et al. (2022), where star-shapedness is deﬁned as
convexity at 0 with r(0) = 0 . This speciﬁc term, concave-star-shapedness, is largely a bsent
from the existing literature related to acceptability indi ces. Historically, star-shapedness
originated in geometry as a property of sets or polygons (Han sen et al., 2020), a context
in which a concave counterpart lacks a meaningful deﬁnition . However, such a distinction
becomes essential when the property characterizes functio nals; here, the direction of the
inequality is no longer purely geometric, but a fundamental characterization of the agent’s
behavioral properties.
Remark 4. What we refer to [0, 1]-super-linearity is related to the concept of positive-sub -
homogeneity in the risk measures theory literature. Speciﬁ cally, for λ ≥ 1, the property can
be rewritten as r(λX) ≤ λr(X), for all X ∈ X , (see Frittelli and Gianin (2002a).
5
Remark 5. Cash-concavity is a weaker requirement than the standard co ncavity, as the lat-
ter directly implies cash-concavity. Naturally, cash-concavity itself implies cash-quasiconcavity.
In addition to the above properties ranking metric can be cho sen to satisfy cash addi-
tivity or weaker related properties.
Deﬁnition 3. Let r : X ⊆ L∞ → [0, ∞] be a map. The map r may satisfy:
i) additivity at k ∈ R+: r(X + k) = r(X) + k, X ∈ supp(r);
ii) sub-additivity at k ∈ R+: r(X + k) ≤ r(X) + k, X ∈ X ;
iii) super-additivity at k ∈ R+: r(X + k) ≥ r(X) + k, X ∈ supp(r).
r is cash-superadditive (respectively, cash-subadditive) if it is super-additive (respectively,
sub-additive) at k for all k ∈ R+. r is cash-additive if is both cash-superadditive and
cash-subadditive, i.e., if and only if r(X + k) = r(X) + k for all k ∈ R+ and X ∈ supp(r).
Remark 6. Notice that the deﬁnition of cash-superadditivity is intro duced only for random
variables X ∈ supp(r). This is due to the following argument. Suppose that for some
X ∈ X there exists k ∈ R+ with r(X + k) = 0 . In this situation cash-super-additivity will
never be satisﬁed, since we would have
r(X + k) = 0 ̸≥r(X) + k > 0.
In contrast, for X ∈ supp(r) we would have r(X + k) ≥ r(X) > 0, so that cash-super-
additivity is a property that could be satisﬁed on supp(r).
The following propositions clarify the relationship among the properties discussed above
and sheds light on the lesser-known concave-star-shapedne ss.
Proposition 2. Let r : X ⊆ L∞ → [0, ∞].
i) If r(0) = 0 , then concavity at 0 is equivalent to [0,1]-super-linearity.
ii) If r is concave at 0 and cash-additive, then r is cash-concave, therefore cash-quasiconcave.
Proof. i) Using [0,1]-super-linearity we have, for any λ ∈ [0, 1]:
r(λX + (1 − λ) · 0) = r(λX) ≥ λr(X) = λr(X) + (1 − λ)r(0),
which shows concavity at 0. Conversely, if r is concave at 0 then
r(λX) = r(λX + (1 − λ) · 0) ≥ λr(X) + (1 − λ)r(0) = λr(X).
ii) Using ﬁrst additivity at (1−λ)k > 0, concavity at 0, then additivity again ( r(0+ k) =
r(0) + k) we have, for any λ ∈ [0, 1) and k > 0:
r(λX + (1 − λ)k) = r(λX) + (1 − λ)k ≥ λr(X) + (1 − λ)r(0) + (1 − λ)k
= λr(X) + (1 − λ)r(k);
6
this proves the concavity, therefore quasi-concavity, at a ny k > 0.
2.1. Normalization properties
Let us discuss the implications of diﬀerent normalization c hoices. One could require nor-
malization at k choosing r(k) = k, ∀k ∈ R+, as originally suggested by Cherny and Madan
(2009). For this normalization, the following suﬃcient con dition holds.
Proposition 3. If r satisﬁes additivity at k ∈ R+ and r(0) = 0 , then it is normalized at
k, i.e., r(k) = k.
Proof. By hypothesis,
r(0 + k) = r(0) + k = 0 + k = k,
which establishes that r is normalized at k.
This normalization choice implies that cash-quasiconcavi ty is equivalent to quasicon-
cavity at 0 under cash additivity.
Proposition 4. Let r : L∞ → [0, +∞] be such that r(0) = 0 . Then r is quasiconcave
at 0. In addition, if r is cash-additive, then quasiconcavity at 0 is equivalent to cash-
quasiconcavity.
Proof. If r(0) = 0 then, by deﬁnition, r(λX) ≥ 0 = min {r(X), r(0)} for any λ ∈ [0, 1].
This gives quasiconcavity at 0.
Concerning the equivalence between quasiconcavity at 0 and cash-quasiconcavity, under
cash-additivity, the implication ⇐ is straightforward. For the converse implication let us
consider the following cases.
Case 1: k > 0. Using additivity at k > 0, quasiconcavity at 0, r(0) = 0 , additivity (in the
opposite direction) we have:
r(λX + (1 − λ)k) = r(λX − λk + k)
= r(λ(X − k)) + k additivity at k > 0
≥ min {r(X − k), r(0)} + k quasiconcavity at 0
≥ min {r(X − k) + k, k} r(0) = 0
≥ min {r(X − k + k), k} additivity at k > 0
= min {r(X), r(k)} .
Case 2: k < 0. We want to prove that r(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≥ min {r(X), r(k)}. Since by
monotonicity r(k) = 0 for k < 0 and r takes value in [0, +∞], we have r(X) ≥ 0 so that
min {r(X), r(k)} = min {r(X), 0} = r(k) = 0 .
7
As an alternative to the normalization property r(k) = k, one could consider the
case where r(k) = + ∞ for all k ∈ R+. From a ﬁnancial perspective, the normalization
r(k) = + ∞ characterizes a category of investors for whom risk-free al locations are univer-
sally acceptable and represent the primary component of a po rtfolio. Indeed, under this
assumption, quasiconcavity at k simpliﬁes to the following property:
r(k) = + ∞ and r(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≥ r(X) for any λ ∈ [0, 1], k ∈ R+, (1)
which is a generalization of the star-shapedness in Righi (2 024). In particular, the star-
shapedness in Righi (2024) coincides with quasiconcavity a t 0 and r(0) = + ∞. The
following proposition shows that for non-negative, monoto ne increasing maps, condition
r(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≥ r(X) for any λ ∈ [0, 1], k ∈ R is suﬃcient but not necessary for cash-
quasiconcavity.
Proposition 5. Let r : L∞ → [0, +∞] be a monotone increasing map.
i) If r(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≥ r(X) for all X ∈ X , λ ∈ [0, 1], k ∈ R, then r is cash-
quasiconcave.
ii) If r is quasiconcave at 0 and r(0) = + ∞, then r(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≥ r(X) for all
λ ∈ [0, 1], k ∈ R+.
Proof. i) Cash-quasiconcavity follows trivially since (without a ny assumption)
r(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≥ r(X) ≥ min{r(X), r(k)}.
ii) If r is quasiconcave at 0 and r(0) = ∞ then, using monotonicity, quasiconcavity at
0, r(0) = ∞, we have
r(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≥ r(λX) ≥ min{r(X), r(0)} = r(X) ∀ k ∈ R+,
so that the claim holds.
Another particular normalization choice is taking r(0) = + ∞ as in Rosazza Gianin and Sgarra
(2013) and Righi (2024). If this is assumed, the following re mark further justiﬁes relaxing
the properties to cash-quasi-concavity alone to avoid the c ollapse to trivial cases.
Remark 7. We observe that in case r(0) = + ∞:
i) Concavity at 0 implies that r is trivial, i.e., r(X) = + ∞ for all X ∈ X . In fact, we
would have
r(λX + (1 − λ)0) ≥ λr(X) + (1 − λ)r(0) = + ∞,
that is r(λX) = + ∞ for every X ∈ X , for every λ ∈ [0, 1].
8
ii) If r is quasiconcave at 0, then for every X ≥ 0, P-a.s., then r(X) = + ∞, meaning
that any random payoﬀ almost surely positive is always accep table.
From a ﬁnancial perspective, the normalization r(0) = + ∞ characterizes passive in-
vestors and accumulators, such as those who leave capital id le or follow a static "buy-
and-hold" strategy. In this framework, the status quo serve s as an inﬁnitely acceptable
benchmark, implying that a position with a zero payoﬀ is alwa ys inherently acceptable.
Consequently, these agents consistently prioritize their current holdings over any active
reallocation, as "doing nothing" is viewed as an optimal bas eline.
3. Characterization of ranking metrics
In this section, we characterize ranking metrics with respe ct to a family of acceptance
sets and a family of risk measures. Acceptance sets A are widely used in the theory of risk
measures and acceptability indices to characterize accept able positions (in terms of risk or
performance). When acceptability is indexed by a level x ∈ R+, this leads to a family of
acceptance sets at diﬀerent levels {Ax}x∈R+ where Ax consists of all positions acceptable
at level x. A position is said to be acceptable at level x if and only belongs to Ax. Let us
provide some preliminary deﬁnitions.
Deﬁnition 4. A subset A ⊆ X is:
i) monotone: if X ≤ Y, X ∈ A =⇒ Y ∈ A;
ii) cash-convex: if X ∈ A, k ∈ A ∩ R =⇒ λX + (1 − λ)k ∈ A for any λ ∈ [0, 1].
A family of sets {Ax}x∈R+ is said
i) increasing (decreasing, resp.) monotone: if Ax ⊆ A y when x ≤ y (x ≥ y, resp);
ii) cash-convex: if Ax is cash-convex for any x ∈ R+.
Given a map r : X ⊆ L∞ → [0, ∞], for instance a ranking metric, a natural choice of
acceptance sets is the family of upper level sets
Ar

Lemma 1. Consider r : X ⊆ L∞ → [0, ∞].

ii) We refer to Peri (2012) and Drapeau and Kupper (2013).
Deﬁnition 5. A map ρ : X ⊆ L∞ → R is a risk measure if it satisﬁes:
i) (decreasing) monotonicity: X ≤ Y, X, Y ∈ X =⇒ ρ(X) ≥ ρ(Y ),
ii) cash-quasiconvexity: ρ(λX + (1− λ)k) ≤ max{ρ(X), ρ(k)}, X ∈ X , k ∈ R, λ ∈ [0, 1].
Monotonicity is the most universally accepted property of a risk measure: if a position
is expected to incur greater losses, its risk must be higher. Throughout, when not speciﬁed,
monotonicity is understood as decreasing in the case of risk measures and increasing in the
case of ranking metrics. Cash-quasiconvexity naturally ge neralizes classical quasiconvexity
by requiring that the risk of a mixture between a risky positi on and a deterministic payoﬀ
never exceeds the maximum risk of the two components evaluat ed in isolation. This prop-

Deﬁnition 6. A map ρ : X ⊆ L∞ → R can also satisfy:
i) convexity at k ∈ R: ρ(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≤ λρ(X) + (1 − λ)ρ(k), X ∈ X , λ ∈ [0, 1];
ii) convexity: ρ(λX + (1 − λ)Y ) ≤ λρ(X) + (1 − λ)ρ(Y ), X, Y ∈ X , λ ∈ [0, 1];
iii) quasiconvexity at k ∈ R: ρ(λX + (1 − λ)k) ≤ max {ρ(X), ρ(k)}, X ∈ X , λ ∈ [0, 1];
iv) quasiconvexity: ρ(λX + (1 − λ)Y ) ≤ max {ρ(X), ρ(Y )}, X, Y ∈ X , λ ∈ [0, 1];
v) [0,1]-sublinearity: ρ(λX) ≤ λρ(X), X ∈ X , λ ∈ [0, 1];
vi) additivity at k ∈ R+: ρ(X −k) = ρ(X)+k, X ∈ X ; equivalently, ρ(X +k) = ρ(X)−k,
X ∈ X ;
vii) sub-additivity at k ∈ R+: ρ(X − k) ≤ ρ(X) + k, X ∈ X ; equivalently, ρ(X + k) ≥
ρ(X) − k, X ∈ X ;
viii) super-additivity at k ∈ R+: ρ(X − k) ≥ ρ(X) + k, X ∈ X ; equivalently, ρ(X + k) ≤
ρ(X) − k, X ∈ X ;
If ρ satisﬁes convexity at k for all k ∈ R, ρ is called cash-convex. If ρ satisﬁes quasiconvexity
at k for all k ∈ R, ρ satisﬁes cash-quasiconvexity. If ρ satisﬁes additivity at k (respectively
sub/super-additivity at k) for all k ∈ R, then ρ satisﬁes the well known cash-additivity
(respectively cash-sub/super-additivity).

performance measures (Frittelli et al., 2016b; Peri, 2012) .

4.2.2. Λ-quantiles based ranking metrics
As a novel example, we consider ranking metrics generated by Λ-quantiles, known

ΛVaR(X) := −qΛ(X) = − inf {y ∈ R : P(X ≤ y) > Λ(y)} ,

Λ is decreasing (see Han et al. (2024)).

4.3. Ranking metrics generated by bibliometric indexes
An alternative class of ranking measures is given by the so ca lled scientiﬁc research
measures (SRM) introduced by Frittelli et al. (2016b). This new class has been introduced
in the bibliometric literature as a generalization of sever al bibliometric indices, including
the h-index, the h-alpha-index, the maximum number of citations, the maximum number
of publications etc.
Here, we assume there exists a portfolio with N assets (alternatively, we can think
about a fund of portfolios) whose returns are denoted by X1, . . . Xp, . . . , XN , respectively.
We denote with X(1), . . . X(p), . . . , X(N ) the same portfolio whose components are ranked
in decreasing order. With a slight abuse of notation, we set X(p) := X(p) if p ∈ {1, . . . , N}
and X(p) = 0 if p > N . The sequence X = {X(p)}p∈R+ can be viewed as a random
variable from R+ to R+. Ranking metrics generated by SRM are maps rSRM that provide
diﬀerent ranks based on a preassigned family of performance curves {fx}.
Deﬁnition 10. Let {fx}x>0 be a family of performance curves such that fx : R+ → R+ for
any x > 0, such that fx is increasing and left continuous on x. Ranking metrics generated
by SRM are maps rSRM : X ⊆ L∞(R+) → [0, ∞] deﬁned as follows
rSRM(X) := sup {x > 0 : X(p) ≥ fx(p) for all p ∈ R+} . (9)
21
In this context, performance curves indicate the return ass ociated with each ranked
asset that is required to achieve a portfolio’s performance level x. The higher the portfolio’s
performance, the higher the performance curve fx that it can reach, and the higher the
corresponding level x. In practice, levels can be taken as integers, i.e. x ∈ N.
Diﬀerent choices of {fx}x>0 provide diﬀerent perfomance/ranking measures. For in-
stance, the h-index ranking metric is characterized by the performance c urve fx(p) =
x1(0,x](p), where 1(0,x](p) takes value 1 when p ∈ (0, x] and zero otherwise; another trivial
example is the max portfolio return that is characterized by fx(p) = x1(0,1](p). Tables

Lemma 4. The map rSRM deﬁned in (9) is monotone increasing and quasiconcave.
Proof. First, we prove monotonicity. Let X1, X2 ∈ X ⊆ L∞(R+). For X2 ≥ X1 for all p ∈
N we have X2(p) ≥ X1(p) ≥ fx(p) for all p ∈ R+, hence
{x > 0 : X1(p) ≥ fx(p) for all p ∈ R+} ⊆ { x > 0 : X2(p) ≥ fx(p) for all p ∈ R+}
22
This implies rSRM (X2) ≥ rSRM (X1).
Now, we show that rSRM is quasiconcave. It is suﬃcient to show that if rSRM (X1) ≥ x
and rSRM (X2) ≥ x, then rSRM (λX1 + (1 − λ)X2) ≥ x. So, for any i = 1 , 2, we have
sup {x > 0 : Xi(p) ≥ fx(p) for all p ∈ R+} ≥ x. This means, for any ǫ > 0 there exists
xi such that xi ≥ rSRM (Xi) − ǫ ≥ x − ǫ, and Xi(p) ≥ fxi(p) for all p ∈ R+. Since
the performance curves fx are monotone increasing in x by deﬁnition, we have, for any
i = 1 , 2, that Xi(p) ≥ fxi(p) ≥ fx−ǫ(p)for all p ∈ N and for any ǫ > 0. This implies
λX1 + (1 − λ)X2 ≥ fxi(p) ≥ fx−ǫ(p) for all p ∈ N and for any ǫ > 0. Hence, using the left
continuity of fx we obtain rSRM (λX1 + (1− λ)X2) ≥ x and thus rSRM is quasiconcave.

5. Empirical application
Using real-world ﬁnancial and climate risk datasets, this s ection illustrates the prac-
tical relevance of the proposed ranking metrics through emp irical analyses of investment
portfolios and European climate risk insurance markets.

performances, the portfolio manager may select high conﬁde nce levels (e.g., 85%–95%),
focusing on returns in that range. In this context, the thres hold ¯x is set as the 90th
percentile of all returns. Conversely, to assess performan ce slightly above the median—thus
avoiding negative values truncated to zero—the manager may select conﬁdence levels just
over 50% (e.g., 55%–65%), centered at τ = 60%. This enables ranking portfolios based on
their typical performance.

the h-index uses squared performance curves, capturing the smal lest integer h such that
at least h assets each outperform the benchmark by a factor of h or more. The enlarged
view of the top-left subplot in Figure 3 clearly illustrates the h-index performance curves,

P6 all bars exceed the level-13 curve but not the level-14 curve , giving an h-index of 13.
This indicates that at least 13 assets in the portfolio have a verage returns at least 13
times greater than the benchmark return (taken as the minimu m return overall). This
corresponds to roughly 85% of the portfolio constituents.
In contrast, the h2-index (top-right subplot) is constructed comparing the hi stogram
bars with the rectangular-shape curves. P6 has an h2-index of 7, indicating that 7 of
its assets achieve average returns at least 72 times the benchmark. The hα-index (with
α = 0 .5) equals 14, indicating 14 assets with average returns at lea st 0.5 × 14 times the
benchmark. Finally, the w-index of 15 means that P6 contains 15 assets whose maximum
average return performance decreases linearly - starting f rom 15 times the benchmark for
the top-performing asset, 14 times for the next, and so on, do wn to 1 times the benchmark
for the 15th asset.
Additional bibliometric-index-based ranking metrics can be created by modifying the
family of performance curves. A manager can design these cur ves to specify the required
average return of all portfolio constituents - relative to a chosen benchmark - to attain
a given performance level. Exploring such ﬂexible curve spe ciﬁcations oﬀers a promising
direction for future research, enabling more tailored asse ssments of portfolio performance.

Figure 7 illustrates that the optimal portfolios derived by maximizing bibliometric in-
dices tend to be more diversiﬁed than those obtained using tr aditional ﬁnancial ratios. Each
ranking metric guides portfolio construction toward speci ﬁc objectives, with bibliometric-
inspired measures aiming for a speciﬁc distribution of port folio returns across assets. The
90%-99%-Λ quantile optimization strategy build a portfolio which max imize the return at
the 90%-99% quantile level, i.e. the portfolio composition with the highest performance at
90%-99% conﬁdence level. The h-index optimization strategy aims to construct a portfolio
in which the largest possible number of assets q each achieve returns of at least q times the
36
benchmark, capturing both the magnitude and breadth of perf ormance across the asset set.
The h2-index, by contrast, favors portfolios where a small subset of top-performing assets
concentrates the majority of the portfolio return, thus emp hasizing strong performers. The
hα index with α = 0.5 targets a middle ground by identifying the largest number of assets
q for which each asset achieves at least 0.5 · q in average returns. This eﬀectively enforces
a minimum return ﬂoor across the portfolio, oﬀering a balanc e between robustness and
diversiﬁcation. As expected, the asset allocation produce d by the hα index diﬀers from
those derived using the h and h2 indices due to its distinct return threshold logic. The
w-index, on the other hand, aims to construct a portfolio with a linearly decreasing return
distribution-starting from the highest return and taperin g down across assets-thereby en-
couraging a smooth performance gradient across constituen ts. Finally, by modifying the
performance curves fx in formula (9), one can design a variety of performance measu res
that shape the distribution of returns across portfolio ass ets in targeted ways. This ﬂexibil-
ity allows for the construction of portfolios that align mor e closely with speciﬁc investment
goals or risk preferences.
6. Conclusion
This paper establishes a uniﬁed axiomatic foundation for ra nking metrics, extend-
ing the classical theory of risk-adjusted performance meas ures and acceptability indices
(Cherny and Madan, 2009; Drapeau and Kupper, 2013; Righi, 20 24). The framework re-
places the traditional reward-per-unit-of-risk interpre tation with a more general notion
of performance rank, formalised through monotonicity and t he newly introduced cash-
quasiconcavity (or quasiconcavity at constants) as concav e counterparty of the quasi-
starshapedness by Han et al. (2024)). This property constit utes a minimal requirement
ensuring that mixing a risky position with a deterministic p ayoﬀ cannot worsen its rank-
ing and captures the fundamental diversiﬁcation intuition underlying most reward–risk
measures.
We further elucidate its link with classical properties, sh owing that for maps normalised
at zero, concavity —and hence quasi-concavity — at zero is eq uivalent to [0-1]-super-
linearity, while cash-additivity bridges these local prop erties to cash-concavity and cash-
quasiconcavity. Within this structure, any ranking metric admits dual representations via
cash-convex acceptance sets or cash-quasiconvex risk meas ures, providing a broad frame-
work encompassing traditional ratios (RAROC, GLR, Omega) a s well as new, monetary
and non-monetary performance measures. Beyond known measur es, this framework intro-
duces new families of performance metrics derived from expe cted-loss/ certainty-equivalent,
and Λ-quantile risk measures, and, notably, a novel class of bibl iometric-based ranking
metrics inspired by the h-index and its extensions. These bibliometric metrics prov ide an
entirely new approach to portfolio performance evaluation compared to traditional ratio-
based assessments by focusing on the distributional charac teristics of portfolio constituents
rather than their risk adjusted mean performance. Empirica l analyses on portfolio per-
37
formance and climate-risk resilience demonstrate the fram ework’s practical relevance and
reveal that alternative ranking metrics can induce materia lly distinct rankings, implicating
the importance of aligning ranking rules with speciﬁc perfo rmance objectives.
Future research could explore bibliometric implementatio ns using diﬀerent portfolio
statistics and studying the relationship between h-index-based and quantile-based metrics,
design portfolio optimisation strategies that maximise tr aditional ratios while controlling
for bibliometric indices, and, extending the framework to d ynamic, robust, or multivariate
settings to further enhance its applicability to ESG-orien ted decision environments.
